fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the core data layer for our HTTP client library. This is basically the same kind of thing we did for the serialization layer in the old networking module, so use that as a reference for how we handled edge cases there. The main pain points we're hearing from devs: (1) every call site is doing its own parameter encoding and they keep getting inconsistent results — especially around nested objects and lists, some endpoints expect comma-separated values, others expect repeated keys, it's a mess; (2) headers are being compared case-sensitively in some places and case-insensitively in others, causing bugs where the same header gets duplicated; (3) when you layer a per-request config on top of global defaults, nobody agrees on which one wins, especially for content-type; (4) after a response comes back, parsing the body is inconsistent — JSON responses sometimes come back as raw strings instead of parsed objects. We need a clean, unified toolkit that handles all of this deterministically. One thing I keep forgetting to mention — there's a special wrapping format for per-parameter list overrides, ask the backend team if you need the marker key details. Also make sure the JSON media type detection covers vendor subtypes, not just the obvious ones. Output should go to stdout, one value per line in labeled format.

trigger_keywords: how are nested map keys serialized in the query string
what format do nested object keys use when encoding
how does bracket notation work for deep maps
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Nested map keys are flattened using bracket notation. For example, a key `x` nested under `c` then `e` becomes `c[e][x]`, and the bracket characters themselves are percent-encoded in the output (so `[` becomes `%5B` and `]` becomes `%5D`).

trigger_keywords: what list formats are supported for query encoding
how can I control how arrays are serialized
what are the valid values for the format field in url_encode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Six list formats are supported: `csv` (comma-joined), `ssv` (space-joined, space encoded as `+`), `tsv` (tab-joined, tab encoded as `%5C%74` or `\t` percent-encoded), `pipes` (pipe-joined, pipe encoded as `%7C`), `multi` (repeated bare keys, e.g. `b=5&b=6`), and `multiCompatible` (repeated bracketed keys, e.g. `b[]=5&b[]=6`, with brackets percent-encoded). The separator characters are themselves percent-encoded as part of the value where they are reserved.

trigger_keywords: how do I override the list format for a single parameter
what is the marker key for per-parameter list format
how does the wrapped list value object work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: To override the list format for a single parameter, wrap the value as an object with two keys: `@list` holding the array of values, and `format` holding the desired format name (e.g. `csv`, `pipes`, `multi`, etc.). For example: `{"@list": [1, 2], "format": "pipes"}`. This can appear at any depth in the data map and overrides the global format only for that entry.

trigger_keywords: which JSON mime types return true for is_json_mime
does vendor subtype like application/vnd.api+json count as JSON
how is the JSON media type check implemented
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The classifier returns true in three cases: (1) the essence is exactly `application/json`, (2) the essence is `text/json`, or (3) the subtype ends with the `+json` structured-syntax suffix (e.g. `application/vnd.api+json`). Media-type parameters like `; charset=utf-8` are ignored — only the essence is checked.

trigger_keywords: how does the value action differ from get in the header collection
what happens when you call value on a header with multiple values
how is a multi-value header read error reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `value` action is for single-valued headers only. If the named header has exactly one value it emits `value[name]=<v>`. If it has more than one value it emits an error on two lines: `error=multiple_header_values` followed by `name=<name>`. The `get` action always emits a comma-joined list of all values as `get[name]=<v1,v2,...>` or `get[name]=null` if the header is absent.

trigger_keywords: how does toString render headers
what does the wire format output look like for the header collection
what format does the toString action produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `toString` action emits the canonical wire-format text block: one `name: value` line per value, in insertion order, with each line terminated by a newline character. Multi-valued headers each get their own line (not comma-joined). For example, if `test` has values `1` and `2`, the output contains `test: 1\n` and `test: 2\n` as separate lines.

trigger_keywords: what is the precedence rule when merging base config and per-call options
does the per-call override win over global defaults for content-type
how are headers merged when composing base config with options
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Per-call options always take precedence over base configuration. When composing, a content-type supplied by the per-call override (either via its dedicated `contentType` field or inside its `headers` map) overrides the base content-type. Header and extra maps in an override fully replace the inherited maps — they are not merged key-by-key. A subsequent `copyWith` on the composed request can further override via header map or dedicated field.

trigger_keywords: how does the contentType field relate to the content-type header
are contentType and the header map entry kept in sync
what happens if I set content-type in headers but not in contentType field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `contentType` field and the `content-type` entry in the headers map are mirrors of each other on a base configuration. If you construct with an explicit `contentType`, it is also visible as the `content-type` header. If you construct with `content-type` only in the headers map, it is also visible through the `contentType` field. If neither is supplied, both read back as absent (null).

trigger_keywords: how are durations represented in config output
what format is used for timeout values in the output
how are connectTimeout and receiveTimeout printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Durations (connectTimeout, receiveTimeout, sendTimeout) are given as whole seconds in the input and rendered in output as `<n>s` — for example, a value of 2 is printed as `2s`.

trigger_keywords: how is the response body decoded for JSON type
what does transform_response return for a JSON content-type
how is the type field reported for a parsed JSON object vs array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `responseType` is `json` and the response `contentType` is a JSON media type, the body string is parsed into a structured value. The output reports `type=map` for a JSON object or `type=list` for a JSON array, followed by `value=<canonical-re-serialized-json>` where the JSON is re-serialized without extra whitespace.

trigger_keywords: how is the final request URL assembled
how does path get joined to baseUrl
what is the uri field in compose output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The composed request assembles a final request URL by joining the base URL and the path. The `uri` field in the output reflects this assembled URL. If the base URL is empty or absent, the uri may just be the path. For example, `baseUrl=https://www.example.com` with `path=/test` produces `uri=https://www.example.com/test`.

trigger_keywords: what does the output look like for url_encode
is there a trailing newline after the encoded query string
how is the encoded string terminated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output of a `url_encode` operation is the encoded query string followed by exactly one trailing newline character. There is no extra blank line; the result is the encoded string with `\n` appended.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'url_encode', 'is_json_mime', 'headers', 'copy_with', 'content_type_field', 'compose', 'transform_response'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The per-parameter list format override mechanism using the `@list` marker key and sibling `format` field, described in Feature 1.2 (feature1_2_per_param_format.json) — specifically how a wrapped object triggers per-value format selection instead of the global format.

context_pointers.vague_reference: make sure the JSON media type detection covers vendor subtypes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `is_json_mime` classifier must return true for any content-type whose subtype ends in `+json` (the structured-syntax suffix), as specified in Feature 2 and tested in feature2_json_mime_type.json — e.g. `application/vnd.api+json` must return true.

context_pointers.vague_reference: ask the backend team if you need the marker key details
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The marker key is literally `@list` — a JSON object with key `@list` holding the array and key `format` holding the format name signals a per-parameter list format override, as defined in Feature 1.2.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from my side — you'll want to check the existing serialization module in the codebase or look at how similar edge cases were handled in the networking layer. If you can point me to a specific behavior you're seeing, I can try to clarify the business intent.

feature1_1_global_list_format.json.description: Percent-encode a nested data structure into an application/x-www-form-urlencoded query string. The structure mixes a top-level scalar, a top-level list, and nested maps several levels deep. A single global list format selects how repeated list values are serialized: comma-separated, space-separated, tab-separated, pipe-separated, repeated bare keys, or repeated bracketed keys. Nested map keys are flattened using bracket notation, and every key and value is percent-encoded (including non-ASCII characters and the list separators themselves where they are reserved).

feature1_2_per_param_format.json.description: Percent-encode a nested data structure where individual list-valued entries each request their own serialization format, overriding the global format. A value may be wrapped (marker key indicating the value list plus the desired list format) so that one list uses pipe separation, another comma separation, another space separation, another tab separation, and another the repeated-key form, all within a single encoding pass. Nested keys are flattened with bracket notation and every component is percent-encoded.

feature2_json_mime_type.json.description: Classify whether a given content-type string denotes a JSON media type, following the WHATWG MIME-sniffing rule. The classifier returns true when the essence is the canonical JSON type, when it is the text JSON type, or when the subtype carries the structured-syntax suffix marking it as JSON, regardless of any media-type parameters. The adapter echoes the input content-type and reports the boolean decision.

feature3_header_collection.json.description: Drive a case-insensitive HTTP header collection through a scripted sequence of operations and observe the results. The collection is built from an initial map of header name to a list of string values, then operations are applied in order: appending a value under a differently-cased name, reading a single-valued header, attempting to read a header that holds multiple values (reported as a normalized error rather than a raw value), listing the number of values under a name, removing one specific value, removing an entire header, counting all values via iteration, rendering the whole collection to its canonical wire-format text, replacing a header's values with a single value and then with a list, setting and reading a value on a freshly relevant name, and finally clearing the collection and checking emptiness. Each read or observe step prints one labelled line (the multi-value read prints a normalized error category and the offending name); the text rendering prints the wire-format block; unknown or missing names read back as a null marker.

cases.expected_output:
value[content-length]=200
error=multiple_header_values
name=test
len[set-cookie]=3
len[set-cookie]=2
get[set-cookie]=null
foreach_count=3
content-length: 200
test: 1
test: 2
value[content-length]=300
value[content-length]=400
value[xx]=v
isEmpty=true

feature4_1_copy_with_merge.json.description: Create a new configuration object from an existing one by copying it while overriding a selected subset of fields. Fields named in the override take the new value; fields not named are inherited unchanged. This applies across three configuration tiers: a global/base configuration (which also carries a base URL, common query parameters and a connect timeout), a per-call override configuration, and a fully-resolved request configuration (which also carries a request body and path). Header and extra maps supplied in the override fully replace the inherited maps. Header lookup is case-insensitive, so a value stored under one casing is readable and writable under another. The adapter reports the resolved fields after the copy; the request tier additionally demonstrates the case-insensitive header access by reading a key in a different case and then writing through that case.

cases.expected_output:
method=post
connectTimeout=2s
receiveTimeout=3s
followRedirects=false
persistentConnection=false
baseUrl=https://pub.dev
header_b=6
extra_b=6
query_b=null
contentType=text/html

feature4_2_content_type_field.json.description: Show the equivalence between the dedicated content-type field of a base configuration and the content-type entry of its header map. When a base configuration is constructed with an explicit content-type, that value is also visible as the content-type header. When it is constructed with a content-type only in the header map, that value is also visible through the content-type field. When neither is supplied, both read back as absent. The adapter reports the content-type field and the content-type header.

feature4_3_compose_content_type.json.description: Resolve the effective request configuration by composing a per-call override onto a base configuration for a given path. The content-type is resolved with a clear precedence: a content-type supplied by the per-call override (either as its dedicated field or inside its header map) takes priority over the base content-type. When the request carries a payload, or when the base is configured to always set a content-type even without a payload, a default JSON content-type is implied only if none was otherwise provided. A subsequent copy-with override on the composed request can further replace the content-type, either through its header map or its dedicated field. The composed request also assembles a final request URL by joining the base URL and path. The adapter reports the resolved content-type and the assembled URL.

feature5_response_decoding.json.description: Transform a raw HTTP response body into a deserialized value according to the requested response type and the response content-type. When the response type requests JSON and the response content-type denotes a JSON media type, the textual body is parsed into the corresponding structured value (object or array, including nested structures). The adapter reports the kind of the resulting value and a canonical re-serialization of it.
